question: A particle is projected with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. A damping force is acting on the particle which is proportional to the square of the distance from the origin i.e. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The distance at which the particle stops:
solution:
F=-αx2

ma=-αx2

a=-αx2 m

vdvdx=-αmx2

∫v00vdv=∫0x-αmx2dx

v22v00=-αmx330x

-v022=-αmx33

x=3mv022α13

subject: Physics
q: The ranges and heights for two projectiles projected with the same initial velocity at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>42</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>H</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. Choose the correct option:
question: The ranges and heights for two projectiles projected with the same initial velocity at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>42</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>H</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. Choose the correct option:
solution: Range<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>u</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mi>g</mi></mfrac></math> and same for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>θ</mi></math>. So, same for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>42</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo>°</mo></math> Maximum height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>u</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>g</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, so height is high for higher <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>. So, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo>°</mo></math> is higher than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>42</mn><mo>°</mo></math>.

subject: Physics
q: Two stones are projected from the top of a cliff $\mathrm{h}$ metres high, with the same speed $\mathrm{u}$, so as to hit the ground at the same spot. If one of the stones is projected at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal then the $\theta$ equals
question: Two stones are projected from the top of a cliff $\mathrm{h}$ metres high, with the same speed $\mathrm{u}$, so as to hit the ground at the same spot. If one of the stones is projected at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal then the $\theta$ equals
solution:
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{u} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{~h}}{\mathrm{~g}}}=(\mathrm{u} \cos \theta) \times \mathrm{t}$
$t=\frac{1}{\cos \theta} \sqrt{\frac{2 h}{g}}$

subject: Physics
q: A bomb is dropped by a fighter plane flying horizontally. To an observer sitting in the plane, the trajectory of the bomb is a :
question: A bomb is dropped by a fighter plane flying horizontally. To an observer sitting in the plane, the trajectory of the bomb is a :
options: straight line vertically down the plane
parabola in a direction opposite to the motion of plane
parabola in the direction of motion of plane
solution: The bomb after being dropped from the plane will have only horizontal velocity but zero vertical velocity. This scenario is as same as if a body is horizontally projected from the top of a tower, and it hits the ground tracing a parabolic path. Hence, the trajectory of the bomb is also a parabola, in the direction of motion of the plane. But if we look the trajectory with respect to observer sitting in the plane then this looks like straight line vertically down the plane due to same horizontal velocity.

subject: Physics
q: A projectile can have the same range $R$ for two angles of projection. If $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the time of flights in the two cases, then the product of the two time of flights is directly proportional to
question: A projectile can have the same range $R$ for two angles of projection. If $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the time of flights in the two cases, then the product of the two time of flights is directly proportional to
solution:
Range is same for complimentary angles. $\mathrm{T}_1=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$ and $\mathrm{T}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin (90-\theta)}{\mathrm{g}}$ and $R=\frac{u^2 \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$
$$
\therefore \mathrm{T}_1 \mathrm{~T}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}} \times \frac{2 \mathrm{u} \cos \theta}{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{g}} \text {. }
$$

subject: Physics
q: Two balls with same mass and initial velocity, are projected at different angles in such a way that maximum height reached by first ball is 8 times higher than that of the second ball. $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the total flying times of first and second ball, respectively, then the ratio of $T_1$ and $T_2$ is :
question: Two balls with same mass and initial velocity, are projected at different angles in such a way that maximum height reached by first ball is 8 times higher than that of the second ball. $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the total flying times of first and second ball, respectively, then the ratio of $T_1$ and $T_2$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, }\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_1=8 \times\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta_1}{2 \mathrm{~g}}=8 \times \frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta_2}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta_1=2 \sqrt{2} \sin \theta_2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta_1 / \mathrm{g}}{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta_2 / \mathrm{g}}=\frac{\sin \theta_1}{\sin \theta_2}=2 \sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A person can throw a ball upto a maximum range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. How high above the ground he can throw the same ball?
question: A person can throw a ball upto a maximum range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. How high above the ground he can throw the same ball?
solution:
Horizontal range is maximum if the angle of projection is 45° and is given by Rmax=u2g

Here, Rmax=100 m

Then, 100=u2g  ⋯1

If the ball is thrown vertically upward, then the ball will attain the maximum height from the ground as Hmax=u22g

Hmax=1002=50 m

subject: Physics
q: A particle moving in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with uniform speed takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> to complete one revolution. If this particle is projected with the same speed at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> to the horizontal, the maximum height attained by it is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The angle of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is then given by :
question: A particle moving in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with uniform speed takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> to complete one revolution. If this particle is projected with the same speed at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> to the horizontal, the maximum height attained by it is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The angle of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is then given by :
solution:
Time period of circular motion is given by, 

2πRv=T⇒v=2πRT

Now, maximum height H=v2sin2θ2g

⇒4R=4π2R2T2sin2θ2g

⇒sinθ=2gT2π2R

⇒θ=sin−12gT2π2R12

q: A small block slides down from the top of hemisphere of radius R=3 m as shown in the figure. The height h at which the block will lose contact with the surface of the sphere is m. (Assume there is no friction between the block and the hemisphere)
question: A small block slides down from the top of hemisphere of radius R=3 m as shown in the figure. The height h at which the block will lose contact with the surface of the sphere is m. (Assume there is no friction between the block and the hemisphere)
solution:
mgcosθ=mv2R   ...(1)

cosθ=hR

Energy conservation

mg{R-h}=12mv2  ...(2)

 from (1) and (2) ⇒mghR=2mg{R-h}R

h=2R3=2 m

subject: Physics
q: A body is projected from the ground at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal. Its velocity after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The maximum height reached by the body during its motion is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>. (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A body is projected from the ground at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal. Its velocity after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The maximum height reached by the body during its motion is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>. (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
After 2 s the velocity is 20 m s-1

⇒vx2+vy2=v2=400

⇒u2cos245°+usin45°-gt2=u22+u2-202=400

⇒u22+u22+400-402u=400

 ⇒u2=402u⇒u=402 m s-1

Now the maximum height of the projectile is given by,

H=u2sin2θ2g⇒H=160020×2×2=20 m

question: Two particles are projected from the same point with the same speed u such that they have the same range R, but different maximum heights, <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Which of the following is correct?
solution:
We get same range for angle of projections θ and  90-θ .
h=u2sin2θ2g
⇒h1=u2sin2θ2g,h2=u2cos2θ2g
h1h2=u4sin2θcos2θ4g2=4u4sin2θcos2θ16g2
=u2sin2θg2116=R216
∴    R2=16h1h2

q: The maximum vertical height to which a man can throw a ball is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>136</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum horizontal distance upto which he can throw the same ball is
question: The maximum vertical height to which a man can throw a ball is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>136</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum horizontal distance upto which he can throw the same ball is
solution:
Maximum height is achieved when the projection is along the vertical direction,

Hmax=v22 g=136 m

Now, for maximum range, angle of projection should be 45°. Therefore,

Rmax=v2 g=2Hmax

=2136

=272 m

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles are fired from ground with same initial speeds from same point at angles $\left(45^{\circ}+\alpha\right)$ and $\left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)$ with horizontal direction. The ratio of their times of flights is
question: Two projectiles are fired from ground with same initial speeds from same point at angles $\left(45^{\circ}+\alpha\right)$ and $\left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)$ with horizontal direction. The ratio of their times of flights is
solution:
$\theta_1=45+\alpha ; \theta_2=45-\alpha$
Time of flight, $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \mathrm{v} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{\sin (45+\alpha)}{\sin (45-\alpha)}$
$\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \alpha+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \alpha}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \alpha-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \alpha}$
$\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}{\cos \alpha-\sin \alpha}=\frac{1+\tan \alpha}{1-\tan \alpha}$

sinθ=12

θ=45°

tanθ=mv2rmg=v2rg=1

v2=rg

v=rg

subject: Physics
q:
A boy throws a ball into air at $45^{\circ}$ from the horizontal to land it on a roof of a building of height $H$. If the ball attains maximum height in 2 s and lands on the building in 3 s after launch, then value of $H$ is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
question:
A boy throws a ball into air at $45^{\circ}$ from the horizontal to land it on a roof of a building of height $H$. If the ball attains maximum height in 2 s and lands on the building in 3 s after launch, then value of $H$ is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
solution:
For projectile motion at 45°, vertical component of initial velocity:
$v_{0y} = v_0 \sin 45° = \frac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}}$
Maximum height occurs at $t = 2$ s when $v_y = 0$:
$0 = v_{0y} - gt$
$\Rightarrow v_{0y} = 10 \times 2 = 20$ m/s
Thus $\frac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}} = 20$, so $v_0 = 20\sqrt{2}$ m/s
Height at landing time $t = 3$ s:
$H = v_{0y}t - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 = 20 \times 3 - \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 9$
$= 60 - 45 = 15$ m

subject: Physics
q:
A boy ties a stone of mass 100 g to the end of a 2 m long string and whirls it around in a horizontal plane. The string can withstand the maximum tension of 80 N. If the maximum speed with which the stone can revolve is Kπ rev min-1. The value of K is :
(Assume the string is massless and un-stretchable)
question:
A boy ties a stone of mass 100 g to the end of a 2 m long string and whirls it around in a horizontal plane. The string can withstand the maximum tension of 80 N. If the maximum speed with which the stone can revolve is Kπ rev min-1. The value of K is :
(Assume the string is massless and un-stretchable)
solution:
The maximum angular velocity of the stone is ω=rpm×2π60=K30 rad s-1

The tension in the string will provide the required centripetal force. Therefore,

T=mω2l⇒80=0.1×K302×2⇒K2=360000⇒K=600

subject: Physics
q: A river of width 200 m is flowing from west to east with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. A boat, moving with speed of $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ in still water, is made to travel one-round trip (bank to bank of the river). Minimum time taken by the boat for this journey and also the displacement along the river bank are $\_\_\_\_$ and $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
question: A river of width 200 m is flowing from west to east with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. A boat, moving with speed of $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ in still water, is made to travel one-round trip (bank to bank of the river). Minimum time taken by the boat for this journey and also the displacement along the river bank are $\_\_\_\_$ and $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
solution:
Width of the river $d = 200$ m.
Speed of river $v_r = 18$ km/h $= 18 \times \frac{5}{18} = 5$ m/s.
Speed of boat in still water $v_b = 36$ km/h $= 36 \times \frac{5}{18} = 10$ m/s.
For minimum time to cross the river, the boat must head perpendicular to the river flow (at $90^\circ$ to the bank).
Time taken to cross once $t = \frac{d}{v_b} = \frac{200}{10} = 20$ s.
For a round trip (bank to bank and back), the total minimum time is $T = 2t = 2 \times 20 = 40$ s.
During the first crossing, the drift (displacement along the bank) is $x_1 = v_r \times t = 5 \times 20 = 100$ m.
During the return trip, to minimize time, the boat again heads perpendicular to the flow relative to the water. The river continues to flow in the same direction, so the drift is again in the same direction as the first trip.
Return drift $x_2 = v_r \times t = 5 \times 20 = 100$ m.
Total displacement along the river bank $= x_1 + x_2 = 100 + 100 = 200$ m.
Thus, minimum time is $40$ s and displacement is $200$ m.

subject: Physics
q: If $t_1$ and $t_2$ are the times of flight of two particles having the same initial velocity $u$ and range $\mathrm{R}$ on the horizontal, then $\mathrm{t}_1^2+\mathrm{t}_2^2$ is equal to
question: If $t_1$ and $t_2$ are the times of flight of two particles having the same initial velocity $u$ and range $\mathrm{R}$ on the horizontal, then $\mathrm{t}_1^2+\mathrm{t}_2^2$ is equal to
solution:
$\mathrm{t}_1=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \alpha}{\mathrm{g}}, \mathrm{t}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \beta}{\mathrm{g}}$ where $\alpha+\beta=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore \mathrm{t}_1^2+\mathrm{t}_2^2=\frac{4 \mathrm{u}^2}{\mathrm{~g}^2}$

subject: Physics
q: A body is projected at $t=0$ with a velocity $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The radius of curvature of its trajectory at $t=1$ s is $R$. Neglecting air resistance and taking acceleration due to gravity $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$, the value of $R$ is:
question: A body is projected at $t=0$ with a velocity $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The radius of curvature of its trajectory at $t=1$ s is $R$. Neglecting air resistance and taking acceleration due to gravity $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$, the value of $R$ is:
solution: Horizontal component of velocity $\mathrm{v}_{x}=10 \cos 60^{\circ}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ vertical component of velocity $v_{y}=10 \cos 30^{\circ}=5 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ After $\mathrm{t}=1 \mathrm{sec}$ Horizontal component of velocity $\mathrm{v}_{x}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Vertical component of velocity $v_{y}=|(5 \sqrt{3}-10)| \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}=10-5 \sqrt{3}$ Centripetal, acceleration $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}^{2}+\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}}=\frac{25+100+75-100 \sqrt{3}}{10 \cos \theta}$ From figure (using (i)) $\tan \theta=\frac{10-5 \sqrt{3}}{5}=2-\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta=15^{\circ}$ $\mathrm{R}=\frac{100(2-\sqrt{3})}{10 \cos 15}=2.8 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q: A swimmer wants to cross a river from point A to point B. Line AB makes an angle of 30° with the flow of the river. The magnitude of the velocity of the swimmer is the same as that of the river. The angle θ with the line AB should be _______°, so that the swimmer reaches point B.
question: A swimmer wants to cross a river from point A to point B. Line AB makes an angle of 30° with the flow of the river. The magnitude of the velocity of the swimmer is the same as that of the river. The angle θ with the line AB should be _______°, so that the swimmer reaches point B.
solution: Both velocity vectors are of same magnitude therefore resultant would pass exactly midway through them θ=30°

subject: Physics
q: An object is projected with kinetic energy $K$ from a point $A$ at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The ratio of the difference in kinetic energies at points $B$ and $C$ to that at point $A$ (see figure), in the absence of air friction is :
question: An object is projected with kinetic energy $K$ from a point $A$ at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The ratio of the difference in kinetic energies at points $B$ and $C$ to that at point $A$ (see figure), in the absence of air friction is :
solution:
At A: $KE_A = K$, $v_A = v$, angle $= 60°$.
At B (highest point): $v_B = v\cos 60° = v/2$, so $KE_B = K/4$.
At C (same level as A): by energy conservation, $KE_C = K$.
$|KE_B - KE_C| = |K/4 - K| = 3K/4$.
Ratio $= \frac{3K/4}{K} = \frac{3}{4}$, i.e., $3:4$.

subject: Physics
q: A stone of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi>kg</mi></math> is tied to end of a massless string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the breaking tension of the string is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then maximum linear velocity, the stone can have without breaking the string, while rotating in horizontal plane, is:
question: A stone of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi>kg</mi></math> is tied to end of a massless string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the breaking tension of the string is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then maximum linear velocity, the stone can have without breaking the string, while rotating in horizontal plane, is:
solution:
In horizontal circle, tension towards the centre of the circular path at any moment will provide the required centripetal force.

Therefore,

Tmax=mv2r⇒400=1×v21⇒v=20 m s-1

The maximum range of a bullet fired from a toy pistol mounted on a car at rest is $R_0=40 \mathrm{~m}$. What will be the acute angle of inclination of the pistol for maximum range when the car is moving in the
direction of firing with uniform velocity $\mathrm{v}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ on a horizontal surface? $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$

direction of firing with uniform velocity $\mathrm{v}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ on a horizontal surface? $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving with constant speed in a circular path. When the particle turns by an angle 90°, the ratio of instantaneous velocity to its average velocity is π:x2. The value of x will be
question: A particle is moving with constant speed in a circular path. When the particle turns by an angle 90°, the ratio of instantaneous velocity to its average velocity is π:x2. The value of x will be
solution:
In circular motion with constant speed, magnitude of instantaneous velocity, vins=ωr.

Time taken to turn by 90° will be =π2ω=π2ω.

Displacement will be equal to the length of  AB=r2+r2=r2

Average velocity

vavg=displacementtime=r2×2ωπ=22πωr

Hence, required ratio vins vavg =ωrπ22ωr=π22

Therefore, x=2.

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving along a circular path with a constant speed of $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. What is the magnitude of the change in velocity of the particle, when it moves through an angle of $60^{\circ}$ around the centre of the circle?
question: A particle is moving along a circular path with a constant speed of $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. What is the magnitude of the change in velocity of the particle, when it moves through an angle of $60^{\circ}$ around the centre of the circle?
solution:
Change in velocity,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
|\Delta \overline{\mathrm{v}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{v}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{v}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{2} \cos (\pi-\theta)} \\
=2 \mathrm{v} \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \quad\left(\because\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{1}\right|=\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{2}\right|\right)=\mathrm{v} \\
=(2 \times 10) \times \sin \left(30^{\circ}\right)=2 \times 10 \times \frac{1}{2} \\
=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{array}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A uniform cylinder of mass M and radius R is to be pulled over a step of height a a <R by applying a force F at its centre  'O' perpendicular to the plane through the axes of the cylinder on the edge of the step (see figure). The minimum value of F required is :
question: A uniform cylinder of mass M and radius R is to be pulled over a step of height a a <R by applying a force F at its centre  'O' perpendicular to the plane through the axes of the cylinder on the edge of the step (see figure). The minimum value of F required is :
solution:
Mg1-R-aR2

FR>Mgcosθ R

F>Mgcosθ

F>MgR2-(R-a)2R. 

⇒Mg1-R-aR2

subject: Physics
q: A ball projected from ground at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ just clears a wall in front. If point of projection is $4 \mathrm{~m}$ from the foot of wall and ball strikes the ground at a distance of $6 \mathrm{~m}$ on the other side of the wall, the height of the wall is :
question: A ball projected from ground at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ just clears a wall in front. If point of projection is $4 \mathrm{~m}$ from the foot of wall and ball strikes the ground at a distance of $6 \mathrm{~m}$ on the other side of the wall, the height of the wall is :
solution: As ball is projected at an angle $45^{\circ}$ to the horizontal therefore Range $=4 \mathrm{H}$ or $10=4 \mathrm{H} \Rightarrow \mathrm{H}=\frac{10}{4}=2.5 \mathrm{~m}$ $(\because$ Range $=4 \mathrm{~m}+6 \mathrm{~m}=10 \mathrm{~m})$ Maximum height, $\mathrm{H}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}}$ $\therefore \mathrm{u}^2=\frac{\mathrm{H} \times 2 \mathrm{~g}}{\sin ^2 \theta}=\frac{2.5 \times 2 \times 10}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}=100$ or, $\mathrm{u}=\sqrt{100}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ Height of wall PA $$ \begin{aligned} & =\mathrm{OA} \tan \theta-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{OA})^2}{\mathrm{u}^2 \cos ^2 \theta} \\ & =4-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{10 \times 16}{10 \times 10 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{0}}}=2.4 \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: The maximum height reached by a projectile is $64 \mathrm{~m}$. If the initial velocity is halved, the new maximum height of the projectile is _____ $\mathrm{m}$.
question: The maximum height reached by a projectile is $64 \mathrm{~m}$. If the initial velocity is halved, the new maximum height of the projectile is _____ $\mathrm{m}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{H}_{1 \text { max }}}{\mathrm{H}_{2 \max }}=\frac{\mathrm{u}_1^2}{\mathrm{u}_2^2} \\ & \frac{64}{\mathrm{H}_{2 \max }}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{(\mathrm{u} / 2)^2} \\ & \mathrm{H}_{2 \max }=16 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
